title: Hydration Calculator for Workouts | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Much Water Should You Drink During Exercise?
description: How much water to drink during exercise? Free hydration calculator based on your weight, intensity, and weather. Includes electrolyte recommendations.
seoKeywords: how much water to drink during workout
exercise fluid intake ml per hour
intro: This calculator estimates how much water you should drink before, during, and after exercise based on your body weight, session duration, workout intensity, and environmental conditions. The core formula comes from sweat-rate science: **ml/hr = body weight (kg) × sweat rate factor × intensity multiplier × climate multiplier**. The American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) recommends drinking 400–800 ml per hour during exercise, but individual needs vary widely — a 90 kg athlete training in summer heat can lose 2,500 ml/hr through sweat alone. Use this calculator to dial in your personal hydration plan and avoid the performance-crushing effects of even 2% body-weight fluid loss.
keyTakeaway: **Hydration target (ml/hr) = Body weight (kg) × 6 ml × Intensity factor (1.0–1.5) × Climate factor (1.0–1.3)**. For a 75 kg person doing moderate exercise in warm weather: 75 × 6 × 1.2 × 1.15 ≈ 621 ml/hr — roughly one 600 ml bottle every 60 minutes.
useCases: A 80 kg runner preparing for a summer half-marathon wants to set a drinking schedule every 15–20 minutes to prevent hyponatremia and dehydration during the 2-hour race.
A CrossFit athlete tracking daily fluid intake needs to account for a 45-minute high-intensity WOD in an air-conditioned gym vs. an outdoor box in July heat.
A recreational cyclist going on a 90-minute group ride in 85°F (29°C) weather needs to know how many 750 ml bidons to carry from the start.
A sports coach building a team hydration protocol for soccer players (different weights, 90 min match, possible overtime) in humid Florida conditions.
A gym-goer returning after illness wants to avoid overhydration (hyponatremia) by knowing the upper safe limit per hour for their body weight.
A personal trainer designing a pre-season conditioning plan needs weight-adjusted hydration targets for each athlete to monitor during two-a-day practices.

faq.q: How much water should I drink per hour of exercise?
faq.a: The ACSM recommends 400–800 ml per hour as a general range, but your personal target depends on body weight, intensity, and heat. Use the formula: Weight (kg) × 6 ml × Intensity Factor × Climate Factor. For a 75 kg person doing hard exercise in the heat, that's roughly 900–1,000 ml/hr — nearly double the lower bound of the generic guideline.

faq.q: Does outside temperature really change how much I need to drink?
faq.a: Yes, significantly. NOAA data shows that at 90°F (32°C) with 70% humidity, heat index reaches the 'danger' zone, and sweat rates can increase by 25–30% compared to a 65°F workout. Our climate factor ranges from 1.0 (cool/indoor) to 1.3 (hot and very humid), reflecting this real physiological response backed by sports medicine research.

faq.q: When do I need electrolytes instead of plain water?
faq.a: Electrolytes become essential when exercise exceeds 60 minutes or when you lose more than ~1 liter of sweat (roughly a 1–1.5% drop in body weight). Sweat contains 460–1,840 mg of sodium per liter. Replacing fluid without sodium during long sessions risks hyponatremia — dangerously low blood sodium. Add 250–500 mg of sodium per 500 ml of fluid after the first hour.

faq.q: Is it possible to drink too much water during a workout?
faq.a: Yes. Exercise-associated hyponatremia (EAH) occurs when athletes drink far more plain water than they lose in sweat, diluting blood sodium below 135 mEq/L. It most commonly affects slower endurance athletes (marathoners, triathletes) who have ample time to over-drink. Symptoms include nausea, swelling, and in severe cases, seizures. The fix is to drink to thirst PLUS include sodium, not to maximize fluid intake.

faq.q: How do I know if I'm dehydrated after a workout?
faq.a: The most practical field test is to weigh yourself before and after exercise. Each kilogram of weight lost equals approximately 1 liter of fluid lost. A loss of >2% body weight (1.5 kg for a 75 kg person) indicates significant dehydration. Urine color is a secondary check: pale yellow (lemonade color) is optimal; dark yellow or amber signals dehydration.

faq.q: Should children and teenagers use the same formula?
faq.a: Children have a higher surface-area-to-mass ratio and a less efficient thermoregulatory system, meaning they heat up faster than adults. The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends ~150–250 ml every 20 minutes for children during vigorous activity. The base rate per kg is comparable, but drink intervals should be shorter and electrolyte drinks should be lower in sugar than standard sports drinks.

faq.q: Does caffeine or coffee before a workout count toward my hydration?
faq.a: At moderate doses (1–3 mg/kg body weight, roughly 1–2 cups of coffee), caffeine has a negligible diuretic effect in habitual consumers, according to NIH research. However, high doses (>5 mg/kg) can increase urine output. A pre-workout coffee is not the same as drinking water: factor in plain water for pre-exercise hydration (5–7 ml/kg in the 4 hours prior, per ACSM) separately from caffeinated beverages.

faq.q: What is the best way to time my drinking during exercise?
faq.a: Rather than drinking reactively (only when thirsty), schedule fluid intake. A common protocol is 150–250 ml every 15–20 minutes for moderate exercise, or every 10–15 minutes during high-intensity or hot-weather sessions. Our calculator outputs a 'drink every X minutes' interval based on your total hourly target divided into ~150 ml sips — a volume that's easy to consume while moving.

faq.q: How does body weight affect hydration needs?
faq.a: Larger bodies produce more metabolic heat and typically have higher sweat rates to dissipate it. The linear relationship (ml/hr scales directly with kg) is supported by ACSM guidelines and sweat-rate research. A 95 kg athlete needs roughly 72% more fluid per hour than a 55 kg athlete at the same intensity and climate — that's the difference between one bottle and nearly two bottles per hour.

sources.name: American College of Sports Medicine – Exercise and Fluid Replacement Position Stand

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The calculator combines four evidence-based variables into a single hourly fluid target, then projects totals for your full session.

```
Base rate (ml/hr) = Weight (kg) × 6 ml/kg/hr          ← ACSM moderate-exercise baseline

Adjusted rate     = Base rate × Intensity Factor × Climate Factor

Total session (ml) = Adjusted rate × Duration (hr)

Drink interval (min) = 60 ÷ (Adjusted rate ÷ 150 ml)  ← assuming ~150 ml sips
```

**Intensity Factors** (derived from perceived exertion and metabolic equivalents):
| Intensity Level | MET Range | Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Light (walking, yoga) | 2–3 METs | 1.0 |
| Moderate (jogging, cycling) | 4–6 METs | 1.2 |
| Hard (tempo run, HIIT) | 7–9 METs | 1.35 |
| Maximum (race effort, CrossFit) | 10+ METs | 1.5 |

**Climate Factors:**
| Weather Condition | Temp / Humidity | Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Cool & indoor (A/C) | <65°F / <40% RH | 1.0 |
| Mild & outdoor | 65–75°F / 40–60% RH | 1.1 |
| Warm & humid | 76–85°F / 60–75% RH | 1.15 |
| Hot & very humid | >85°F / >75% RH | 1.3 |

---

## Reference Table — Ready-Made Targets

| Weight (kg) | Moderate / Cool | Moderate / Hot | Hard / Hot |
|---|---|---|---|
| 55 kg | 396 ml/hr | 514 ml/hr | 693 ml/hr |
| 65 kg | 468 ml/hr | 608 ml/hr | 819 ml/hr |
| 75 kg | 540 ml/hr | 702 ml/hr | 945 ml/hr |
| 85 kg | 612 ml/hr | 795 ml/hr | 1,071 ml/hr |
| 95 kg | 684 ml/hr | 889 ml/hr | 1,197 ml/hr |

---

## Typical Cases

**Case 1 — Recreational runner, 70 kg, 45-minute morning jog, mild weather:**
- Base: 70 × 6 = 420 ml/hr
- Adjusted: 420 × 1.2 (moderate) × 1.1 (mild) = 554 ml/hr
- Total session: 554 × 0.75 hr = **415 ml** → carry one 500 ml bottle
- Drink interval: every ~16 minutes

**Case 2 — Competitive cyclist, 85 kg, 2-hour ride, 88°F / 80% humidity:**
- Base: 85 × 6 = 510 ml/hr
- Adjusted: 510 × 1.35 (hard) × 1.3 (hot-humid) = **894 ml/hr**
- Total session: 894 × 2 = **1,788 ml** → carry 2× 750 ml + 1× 500 ml bidon
- Drink interval: every ~10 minutes

**Case 3 — Yoga class, 60 kg, 60 minutes, air-conditioned studio:**
- Base: 60 × 6 = 360 ml/hr
- Adjusted: 360 × 1.0 × 1.0 = **360 ml/hr**
- Total session: 360 ml → one standard water bottle is plenty

---

## Electrolytes

When exercise exceeds **60 minutes** or occurs in hot/humid conditions, plain water is not enough. Sweat contains approximately **20–80 mEq/L of sodium** (roughly 460–1,840 mg/L). The CDC and sports medicine consensus suggest electrolyte replacement when:
- Duration > 60 min
- Sweat loss > 1 liter (approx. 1.5% body-weight drop)
- Temperature > 80°F

A practical guide: for every **500 ml** of fluid consumed past the first hour, add **250–500 mg sodium** (from a sports drink, electrolyte tablet, or salted snack). Potassium (200–400 mg/L lost in sweat) and magnesium are secondary but relevant for cramp prevention in sessions over 90 minutes.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Drinking only when thirsty:** Thirst lags behind actual dehydration by 1–2% body-weight fluid loss — enough to reduce aerobic performance by up to 10% (NIH research). Scheduled drinking beats reactive drinking.

2. **Ignoring pre-exercise hydration:** Arriving at your workout already dehydrated (common after morning coffee) means you're playing catch-up. The ACSM recommends consuming ~5–7 ml/kg in the 4 hours before exercise — that's 375–525 ml for a 75 kg person.

3. **Overhydrating with plain water (hyponatremia):** Drinking excessive plain water during long, slow events (>3 hr marathons, triathlons) dilutes blood sodium below 135 mEq/L — a potentially dangerous condition. Always include electrolytes in sessions over 90 minutes.

4. **Using a single fixed rule (e.g., "8 cups a day"):** Body weight, sweat rate, climate, and intensity all multiply each other. A fixed rule ignores a 2× difference in needs between a 55 kg yoga practitioner and a 95 kg footballer in summer heat.

5. **Not accounting for post-exercise rehydration:** For every kilogram of body weight lost during exercise, drink **1.25–1.5 liters** afterward (ACSM guideline) to fully restore fluid and electrolyte balance within 4–6 hours.

---

example.title: 85 kg athlete, 90-minute HIIT session, hot & humid outdoor gym (88°F / 78% RH)
steps: Step 1 — Base rate: 85 kg × 6 ml/kg/hr = 510 ml/hr
Step 4 — Total session (90 min = 1.5 hr): 895 × 1.5 = 1,343 ml
Step 6 — Electrolytes: session >60 min + hot climate → add ~250–500 mg sodium per 500 ml past first hour (≈500–750 mg extra sodium total)
example.result: Drink ~895 ml/hr — total 1,343 ml for the session. Carry two 750 ml bottles. Sip ~150 ml every 10 minutes. Include electrolytes in bottle #2.
